2604.06375.title: SymptomWise: A Deterministic Reasoning Layer for Reliable and Efficient AI Systems
2604.06375.paper_summary: AI-driven symptom analysis systems face persistent challenges in reliability, interpretability, and hallucination. End-to-end generative approaches often lack traceability and may produce unsupported or inconsistent diagnostic outputs in safety-critical settings. We present SymptomWise, a framework that separates language understanding from diagnostic reasoning. The system combines expert-curated medical knowledge, deterministic codex-driven inference, and constrained use of large language models. Free-text input is mapped to validated symptom representations, then evaluated by a deterministic reasoning module operating over a finite hypothesis space to produce a ranked differential diagnosis. Language models are used only for symptom extraction and optional explanation, not for diagnostic inference. This architecture improves traceability, reduces unsupported conclusions, and enables modular evaluation of system components. Preliminary evaluation on 42 expert-authored challenging pediatric neurology cases shows meaningful overlap with clinician consensus, with the correct diagnosis appearing in the top five differentials in 88% of cases. Beyond medicine, the framework generalizes to other abductive reasoning domains and may serve as a deterministic structuring and routing layer for foundation models, improving precision and potentially reducing unnecessary computational overhead in bounded tasks.
2604.06375.paper_summary_zh: AI 驅動的症狀分析系統在可靠性、可解釋性和幻覺方面面臨持續挑戰。端到端生成方法往往缺乏可追溯性，並且在安全關鍵的環境中可能產生不支持或不一致的診斷輸出。我們提出了 SymptomWise，一個將語言理解與診斷推理分開的框架。該系統結合了專家策劃的醫學知識、確定性編碼驅動的推理，以及對大型語言模型的有限使用。自由文本輸入被映射到經過驗證的症狀表示，然後由一個在有限假設空間上運作的確定性推理模塊進行評估，以生成排名的鑑別診斷。語言模型僅用於症狀提取和可選解釋，而不是用於診斷推理。這種架構改善了可追溯性，減少了不支持的結論，並使系統組件的模塊化評估成為可能。對 42 例專家撰寫的挑戰性小兒神經學案例的初步評估顯示與臨床醫生共識有意義的重疊，正確診斷在 88% 的案例中出現在前五個鑑別中。超越醫學，該框架可以推廣到其他歸納推理領域，並可能作為基礎模型的確定性結構和路由層，改善精確度並可能減少在有限任務中的不必要計算開銷。
2604.06375.author: Isaac Henry et.al.
2604.06375.authors: Isaac Henry, Avery Byrne, Christopher Giza, Ron Henry, Shahram Yazdani
